speaker: interviewee
text: --talking to people there. And though I said I'm not going to do it, somehow I came out and said that I'm a Holocaust survivor. And they said they'll get in touch with me. But they didn't have my address or anything. So they did get in touch with my daughter. And she told me that they called her from the thing. And I told her that I really don't want it. And I don't-- it's gonna be too painful. I don't want to go back on it. And I guess she let it go. But then somebody from around you called me. And I told them the same thing. But they insisted that I should do it. And I finally said that they should call me back. And when they called me back, I said OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wow. That's good. So does-- do your daughters know you're doing it today?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. No. I didn't tell them about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's good. That's good.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't think that it's gonna be really to-- that kind of production. But I'm gonna do my best.

speaker: interviewer
text: Very good.

speaker: interviewee
text: I hope that it'll help somebody.

speaker: interviewer
text: The date is September 30, 1996. The survivor is Joyce Lipper, J-O-Y-C-E L-I-P-P-E-R, maiden name Maslansky, Maslanska, M-A-S-L-A-N-S-K-Y. The interviewer is a Gloria May, G-L-O-R-I-A M-A-Y. We're in Sunrise, Florida, USA. The language is English. This is tape number one. Today is September 30, 1996. I'm here to interview Joyce Lipper, born Maslansky. My name is Gloria May. We're in Sunrise, Florida, USA. And the language is English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much for being here. We'll begin if you can please tell me your full name. My name is Joyce Lipper. My maiden name is Maslansky.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: February 28, 1925.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're how old now?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm going to be 72 in February next year.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm born in Klewan, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: About how big of a town was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Small town. But we had a big Jewish population. We had about four synagogues.

speaker: interviewer
text: About how many people would just say, approximately?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe 5,000 or so.

speaker: interviewer
text: And about what percentage were Jewish then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Quite a big percentage. Because the non-Jewish people mostly lived on the outskirts of town, like farms and stuff. So I would say maybe 55%, 60% were Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did the Jewish people earn their living?

speaker: interviewee
text: Business, mostly.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother had three daughters. My father passed away when I was six months old.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Manechan. And my mother raised three girls by herself. She had a little grocery store in the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Chana.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how about your sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister-- my older sister's name was Shifra. And my younger sister's name was Mina.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were the middle?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was the baby, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was life like for you growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not bad, not bad. I-- we were poor, but I didn't feel it, a lot of family closeness. We had-- my father's family was an American. And they helped us a lot. My older sister, when she became of age, she was-- went to school and learned to be a dressmaker. And she was helping out. And we all did little chores. But it was a happy, good family.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was it that your father's family had come to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's oldest brother went-- came to the United States. I really don't know exactly how. But then after he-- another brother and then a sister. And then they took their mother, they brought over to the United States. So I really had here in the States my father's family when I came.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did your father stay in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: I really couldn't say. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know if the family had thought about immigrating at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister, my older sister, they were planning about it that when she'll be old enough that they gonna take her-- bring her to America, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your father died when you were just a young child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's your fondest memory of your childhood?

speaker: interviewee
text: The family, closeness, had there-- there aunts, uncles, their families. We were very close, taking care of each other. And it was nice. I was young, going to school. And even if I didn't have much, I didn't miss it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to public school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the relationship of the Jewish children to the non-Jewish children?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't mix too much, except in school. But I did have some good friends, non-Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you remember any incidents of anti-Semitism that happened before Hitler came to power?

speaker: interviewee
text: Certain things in town I remember they were talking about. But as I said, I was a young girl. And I really don't remember having to deal with any of that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you remember when Hitler came to power in Germany? Did anything start to change in your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kinds of things?

speaker: interviewee
text: When the Pole-- the war between Poland and Germany, they-- we started to see people from Germany coming our-- to our part. Because we were on the closer to the Russian borders, the Ukraine part of Poland. So there were people coming to our part, special Jewish people. But then supposedly Stalin made the pact with Germany. And we became Russians.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is when the war started?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's when wars.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember exactly when the war started?

speaker: interviewee
text: By us, that was when the war started between the German and the Russians in '41. That's when we really felt it. That's when it-- the trouble started.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened exactly to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the summer, the beginning of the summer, I would say maybe June of '41, the Germans came in. Knowing what to expect from the Germans, we all tried to run away and hide in maybe the small towns. And they were bombing. And a lot of people were in the synagogues hiding. And when they came in that day, they killed 20,000 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans. They bombed the synagogues. We had one big, big synagogue. And there were a lot of people there hiding, because the structure was a sound building, a big one. So they thought that they would be safe there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I ran away to a small town, like a village, where you expected the Germans not to go through right away from the first came in. And I ran away with my mother and my two sisters.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we go to that, and what was the name of the town that you ran to? What was the name of the town you ran to?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was a little village.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was it called?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nowostaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we get there, let's back up a little to before the Germans came to your town. You said you knew what to expect from the Germans. What kinds of things had you heard from 1939 on?

speaker: interviewee
text: We heard from the Polish people, the Jewish Polish people that came to our part of the country, that they kill Jews and they take them to work, hard labor, and all that. And so we knew a lot what was going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember particularly any instant of hearing stories from any particular person?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. Yeah. There were people that came and said that they ran away, but their families were killed and that they bombed the city. I mean, this was a war. So they bombed the city where a lot of people got killed. But when they came in, they [? used ?] [? the ?] special look for the Jewish people. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you believe what they told you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I guess so, when you talk to people that you see that they left everything and were running away. So you have to believe it.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said the Russians were in your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell-- tell me about the time when the Russians were there.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Russians came in when they divided Poland in half with Germany. So life was fine. I mean, there weren't any special luxuries. But we weren't used to luxuries like we know now. And it was life going on. People went to work. I went back to school. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Had anything changed in the school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. We were taught Russian. And they tried to organize the youth organizations. And they gave a lot of attention to young people to try to tell them about Russia. And-- but at that time, being young in school, you didn't mind those things. Because it was fun time. It wasn't bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they treat the Jewish students any different than the non-Jewish students?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know if they had offered citizenship, Russian citizenship, to your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: They wanted the young people to join the [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's the Russian youth organization. And if you-- if they found out there was a lot of people that were telling on people-- let's say they were rich or they had big business-- those are the people that they use to send away to Siberia, take them away, a lot of non-Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know anyone that happened to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. My husband's family had been through that years ago, before the World War II. My husband's father was sent to the Siberia, because they were rich. And they-- when the Russians at that time came in in our area, they sent them away.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we knew a little bit about it. But I knew a lot of people even, as I said, the non-Jewish people were overnight-- get out and just with trains moved out. And nobody knew what happened to them. But I guess-- but after a while, you knew that that's going on. And was your mother still able to run her grocery store the same?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not as much as before, because it wasn't available, the products that she was selling. But we managed, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any thought at this time of leaving the country?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you still able to communicate with the relatives in the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you mentioned your husband. Did you know him already at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was it that you met him? And what is his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went to school together. And I met him in my fifth grade. We were friends. We grew up together. And he was one class higher than I was. And we were friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was Eli.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you think of him as a boyfriend in those days?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, not really. We were kids. And we just hang out together. And in fact, he came from the richest family in the town. And I was one of the poorest. Because a mother-- a woman with three girls by herself wasn't a good thing in Europe at those times.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you join that Communist organization that they wanted the children to join that you were telling me about?

speaker: interviewee
text: We really didn't give a chance to do that. Because, I mean, we were going to school. And they were trying, you know, to get us to get to know the organizations and so on. But then the war started again.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what exactly happened then? Let's go back to that point then.

speaker: interviewee
text: When the Germans came in, our town was bombed. Because we lived on the main road where it was going from the south to the north. And as I said, it's not just that they bombed the town coming in. But that first night, they bombed all the synagogues and killed a lot of people. But they also killed non-Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your first contact with the Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. About a couple of days later, we all came down. We knew what went on there, that were-- people were killed and so on. But we came back to town. And they-- they put us in a-- in one area, the Jewish people. Most of our homes were bombed. There weren't any homes to go to. But we went five, six, maybe even more, families in one house. And it was like a ghetto. But it wasn't under wire or we weren't at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this exactly in 1941? About what month was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say maybe in August or so.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your house had been bombed? Was your house bombed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to take any of your possessions out of your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where did you go to live?

speaker: interviewee
text: As I said, we lived in a house there, that-- the area that they told us that we should stay in. We lived with other people, a lot of people, five, six families.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they relatives of yours?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not really.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was it that you decided which house to go live in?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. You didn't decide. You just went where you had a chance to go, where you had a place to go. A lot of people were-- were killed already. I lost my younger sister. So there were just the three of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: What had happened to your younger sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was killed the first time when they came in.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: It would be in July of '41.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: I really don't know what happened. I know that she got killed. She was-- at the time, she was with the people, where hiding. And they killed all the people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was she hiding?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was in town.

speaker: interviewer
text: With another family?

speaker: interviewee
text: With other people, with my aunt and uncle. And at that time, I lost my cousins. I lost a lot of family.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother, sister, and you were where?

speaker: interviewee
text: We came back to the town. And we settled down like in the area where they wanted us to live and stayed and go to-- went to work every day.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Fixing the roads, lumber yard. There was a plant by us where they were making lumber. Not making lumber, but cutting the lumber trees to-- to boards and things like that. So we work there and every day came home at night and had very little to eat. They made like a Jewish committee that they took care of us. And we used-- they used to give us out bread every day and so on. But it was already-- we saw that it's-- something is coming. Because in September that year, they had got together the rest of the Jewish people. And they said that they-- we heard about it before-- that they're gonna be killing the rest of the Jews. So that's when--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you hear?

speaker: interviewee
text: People were talking. People were talking. But-- and they did certain things. They didn't let you go to work. We found out afterwards that that day, a couple days already, they didn't let you go to work. They wanted you to stay put and so on. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me what other kind of rules there were besides the fact that you had to go to work. Any other rules that you can remember at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, not to go out from the area. Except that you-- when you went to work, you were led. They used to come and ask for a certain amount of people, or men, or women. And they led us to the-- where they wanted us. A lot of times they took us with a bus or some kind of truck or something to work. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: How was it that you were getting food?

speaker: interviewee
text: That we were getting food-- when we went to work, we used to get an extra piece of bread or something else extra. That's why people went to try to go to work. I used to go every day to work. I was a young girl, strong. And I used to go home and bring the extra piece of bread or whatever I got for my family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you said there were five families living together at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe even more.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the sleeping accommodations like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Three in a bed [? also. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were you sharing the bed with?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother and sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there are other people in the room as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. Each corner had a bed.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how about the sanitation facilities? What were they like?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] I don't think there was any to talk about. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you-- you said you didn't take anything from your home. Did you take any valuables of any kind from your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. We weren't rich people. We didn't have much to take. And we didn't have anything. We had the clothes on our back. Some people that knew what's coming and saw it and had things. So they gave it to some non-Jewish people, maybe to hold or-- or put it in their home somewhere where they-- even the home was bombed, they could go in in the-- in the cellar and get it or something.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then in September-- let me ask you before that. Had you thought of trying to leave at any time during this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. We were getting ready for it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you going to go?

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- at that time my friend, my boyfriend, I would say, family, they had means. So they had connections in the big city from our town. And they said that they're gonna go there, thinking that there, they gonna let the Jews live longer or whatever. There was a ghetto already there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the big city?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rowne. That was, like, each state had a capital. Is that what you say? And that was the capital of [NON-ENGLISH]. And at that time, my mother with my sister went to the town where my mother came from. She had her sisters and brothers.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what town was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Alexandria. And I went with my husband-- not at that time, but my boyfriend's-- family. And we went to Rowne.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was in September?

speaker: interviewee
text: About September of '41. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it difficult to leave? Did you have to do something special to get out of your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. You didn't have to have do anything. You just had to make sure that you can get out without being caught, if you were caught on the road going to that city. We walked. It was 26 kilometers from our town to Rowne. If you walked and you got caught, you got killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kinds of things did you do to keep from getting caught?

speaker: interviewee
text: We can talk? Yeah. Dress up or that they shouldn't recognize that you're Jewish. Try not to walk during the day.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We'll stop here to change the tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
